bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Introduced to goalball in 2015 at a camp at Turnstone Center in Fort Wayne, Indiana
  • Has a condition called Stickler syndrome which breaks down all the connective tissue in your body
  • It started with torn and detached retinas and developed into other complications such as glaucoma
  • Parents are Terry and Beth Buhler
  • Has three siblings - Allie, Kameron and Kendall
  • Both his mother-in-law, Karen Helmacy, and father-in-law, Jerry Windell, played goalball and won Paralympic medals
  • Karen was a gold medalist in javelin and goalball in 1984, and Jerry won silver in goalball in 1988
  • Hobbies include playing the drums, working out, and watching football all day on Sundays.
Paralympic Experience
  • 1-time Paralympian
    • Paralympic Games Tokyo 2020, 4th (Team - Men)
More

USABA

bio.education: The Woodlands High School (2021) Stanford University
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Lauren is the daughter of Alexander and Missy Burch
  • Her dad was a diver in high school
  • She has three siblings, Alyssa, Julia and Tyler
  • Lauren enjoys chess, musical theater, singing, cooking, escape rooms, reading
  • Started diving at age 10
  • Her favorite dive is a 107C on 10-meter, and her favorite diver is Laura Wilkinson (she's known her for years and has babysat her kids)
  • Switched from a left-legged hurdle to a right-legged hurdle for 2021, and she also started training more on tower to prepare for college
  • Likes to listen to a variety of music, ranging from Queen to Hamilton, when she is competing (her favorite song is Good Old Fashioned Lover Boy by Queen)
  • Bohemian Rhapsody is her favorite movie, and if she could meet anyone, it would be Freddie Mercury
  • Says she has a very unique style with colorful clothing and jewelry and loves to show off her style no matter where she goes
  • Favorite meal is chicken and waffles from Black Walnut in The Woodlands
  • She would like to become a cardiothoracic surgeon
  • Is involved with the Athletes in Medicine club at Stanford
  • Enjoys playing chess with her family, especially after watching The Queen's Gambit

Quick Facts
  • Drafted No. 14 by the WNBA's Atlanta Dream in 2015
  • Majored in communication studies at the University of Tennessee
  • Her great grandfather, Lloyd Burdick, played for the NFL's Chicago Bears from 1931-32
Career Highlights
  • 3x3 World Cup, Vienna, 2023 (Team - Women)
  • Pan American Games, Santiago, 2023 (Team - Women)
  • 3x3 World Cup, Antwerp, 2022 (Team - Women)
  • 3x3 World Cup, Moscow, 2014 (Team - Women)
  • World Championship U-19, 2011 (Team - Women)
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 bronze)
    • Olympic Games Paris 2024, bronze (Team - Women)

Quick Facts
  • Burdick was born in Utah and spent much of his childhood playing soccer, basketball, swimming and water polo
  • After completing a few semesters as an undergraduate, he enlisted in the Navy because it would afford him opportunities to administer medical treatment during combat operations
  • He was assigned as a hospital corpsman to the 3rd Battalion 6th Marine Regiment based out of Camp Lejeune, North Carolina
  • From 2007 to 2010, he deployed once to Iraq and twice to Afghanistan
  • It was with one week left of his third deployment that Burdick’s armored vehicle was struck by a roadside bomb, resulting in serious injuries to both of his feet
  • He underwent limb salvage and intense physical therapy
  • When he regained strength, Burdick was fitted for a pair of braces that allowed him to stand and walk upright again
  • An avid snowboarder before the accident, he was introduced to the National Ability Center in Park City, Utah, through the Wounded Warrior Project
  • There, he began training with Team Utah, a competitive snowboard team for both able-bodied and adaptive snowboarders
  • He quickly became a strong component of Team USA, competing at the Winter Paralympic Games Sochi 2014
  • Following Sochi, Burdick began developing pain in his legs and made the decision to have both amputated.
  • Burdick is a retired E-5 2nd Class Petty Officer in the United States Navy
  • Has three siblings, Allegra, Amika and Tim
  • Hobbies include biking, sailing, hiking and traveling
  • In addition to snowboarding, Burdick has his private pilot’s license through Salt Lake Community College
  • He has sailed 900 miles across the Gulf of Mexico more than four times
  • He and his brother lived on his family’s sailboat for one year in Florida and another in Texas
  • Competed at the Warrior Games with the U.S. Navy in 2011 and 2012.
Paralympic Experience
  • 2-time Paralympian
    • Paralympic Games Beijing 2022, 14th (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)
    • Paralympic Games Sochi 2014, 8th (Snowboardcross - Standing - Men)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2022 – bronze (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men), 11th (Snowboard Cross Team Event - Men), 13th (Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)
  • Years of participation: Banked Slalom - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men 2017, 2022; Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men 2017, 2022; Snowboard Cross Team Event - Men 2022
  • Medals: 1 (1 bronze)
    • Bronze – 2022 (Snowboard Cross - Lower Limb 1 Impaired - Men)

Quick Facts
  • Bureau took up para-swimming at age 11. A hospital in his hometown of Pittsburgh encouraged him to try a swimming program for children with an impairment. He lost his left hand in a farming accident in 1996. He trains in the pool for two hours every day except Sunday. Three days a week, he lifts weights and has a second swimming session
  • Son of Marjorie and William Bureau
  • Has two sisters, Brittany and Heather, and one brother, Luke
  • Both sisters swam to a top-12 in the Pennsylvania State Athletic Conference

  • Hobbies include many outdoor activities: hiking, fishing, water sports, boating, off-roading and shooting

More
Tim grew up in the small town of Paul Smiths, New York, which is located just outside of the Olympic village of Lake Placid.  With abundant winter snow and an active family, Tim started skiing and competing from a very young age.  After competing in Nordic skiing since childhood, Tim tried biathlon when he was 13 and never looked back.

Along the way, Tim has become a three-time winter Olympian, competing in the 2006 and 2010 games.  At the 2013 World Championships he won the silver medal in the 20 km Individual competition, the second American man ever to win a World Championship medal. Tim has also competed on the World Cup tour for the past ten years and during this time has accumulated multiple podium finishes and numerous top ten’s. During the 2009/2010 season he notched up three podium finishes in December to attain the number one world ranking for a portion of the season. In doing so, Tim became the first American to wear the yellow bib, marking him as the top ranked biathlete in the world.


Tim currently resides and trains out of Lake Placid, New York.  Besides being close to Tim’s hometown, Lake Placid is home to the US Olympic Training Center and is surrounded by ideal training conditions.  As Tim enters the prime of his career, he hopes to continue breaking barriers by becoming the first American to win an Olympic medal in the sport of biathlon.

MORE ON Nikolas: Nikolas began cross country skiing when he was seven years old through the Strider Glider program and years later was introduced to laser biathlon. Nikolas loved the aspect of shooting and began competitively doing biathlon in 2015. Nikolas hobbies include ultimate frisbee and sailing. Nikolas loves to play sports and competes at a high school level in track and field, cross country skiing, soccer and cross country running. Nikolas has been to three Junior National cross country skiing events for the Far West region.

Quick Facts

Chelsea Burns competed in NCAA Division I track and field and cross country at Colgate University while completing a double major in geography and computer science. She was named the Colgate Female Athlete of the Year her senior year. Burns was introduced to triathlon as one of the first members of USA Triathlon’s Collegiate Recruitment Program. She competed her first triathlon a few weeks after graduating college in 2012, then she competed in her first ITU race in 2013 at the Edmonton ITU Triathlon World Cup. Burns is a USA Triathlon certified coach. During her workouts, Burns enjoys listening to sports podcasts. Outside of triathlon she enjoys hiking, trivia, snowboarding, cooking, crossword puzzles and gardening. 

Elite Athlete Career

2019: Finished 16th at the ITU World Triathlon Abu Dhabi, on March 8 • Placed 18th at the ITU World Triathlon Bermuda, on April 27 • Finished in 13th at the ITU World Triathlon Yokohama, on May 18 • Placed 21st at the ITU World Triathlon Leeds, on June 9 • Finished in 15th at the ITU World Triathlon Montreal, on June 29 • Placed 16th at the Hamburg Wasser World Triathlon on July 6

2018: Placed 7th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Cape Town on February 11 • Finished 18th at the ITU World Triathlon Abu Dhabi on March 2, • ITU Triathlon World Cup Mooloolaba on March 10, DNS • Placed 12th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup New Plymouth on March 25 • Finished 17th at the ITU World Triathlon Bermuda on April 28 • Placed 22nd at the ITU World Triathlon Yokohama on May 12 • Finished 10th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Cagliari on June 2 • Placed 19th at the ITU World Triathlon Leeds on June 10 • Earned a 3rd place finish at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Tiszaujvaros on July 7 • Finished 11th at the ITU World Triathlon Montreal on August 25 • Concluded the ITU World Triathlon Series with a 13th-place finish in Gold Coast, Australia, on Sept. 15, taking 22nd in the season-long rankings • Earned a top-10 finish at the Weihai ITU Triathlon World Cup on Sept. 22 • Grabbed a silver medal to close the season at the Miyazaki ITU Triathlon World Cup on Nov. 10

2017: Finished 6th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Cape Town on February 11 • Placed 9th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup on April 2 • Competed in the ITU World Triathlon Gold Coast on April 8, DNF • Finished 5th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Chengdu on May 7 • Earned a 3rd place finish at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Madrid on May 28 • Placed 10th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Cagliari on June 4 • Finished 32nd at the ITU Triathlon World Triathlon Hamburg on July 15 • Placed 10th at the ITU Triathlon World Triathlon Edmonton on July 29 • Competed in the ITU Triathlon World Cup Yucatan on Aug 12, DNF • Earned a 3rd place finish at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Huelva on September 24 • Placed 6th at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Sarasota on October 8 • Finished 21st at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Miyazaki on November 4

2015: Fifth at the Pan American Games on July 11 in Toronto, Ontario • Eighth at the Huatulco ITU World Cup on June 14 • Recorded her career-best ITU World Triathlon Series finish (ninth) on April 25 in Cape Town, South Africa • Competed in the ITU Triathlon World Cup Tiszaujvaros on August 8, DNF • Finished 23rd in the ITU Triathlon World Cup on October 4 • Earned a 5th place finish at the ITU Duathlon World Championships Adelaide on October 17 • Placed 23rd at the ITU Triathlon World Cup Tongyeong on October 24

bio.biography:
Quick Facts

  • Specialty: Parallel Giant Slalom 

  • Began snowboarding at age 10 on the slopes of Mount Shasta Ski Park

  • Made his world cup debut in 2019

  • Competed in the Parallel Giant Slalom at the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, finishing 31st

  • Serves as a hotshot wildland firefighter for the US Forest Service

  • Enjoys baking 

Biographical Information 

Robert Burns is an American snowboarder specializing in parallel giant slalom. Born in Mount Shasta, California, he started snowboarding at the age of 10, developing his skills on the local slopes. After graduating from the University of Idaho in 2012, Burns pursued competitive snowboarding, making his world cup debut in 2019.

At the Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, Burns represented Team USA in the Parallel Giant Slalom event, finishing 31st. Beyond his athletic career, he serves as a hotshot wildland firefighter during the summer months as a part of the Shasta Lake Hotshots, demonstrating his commitment to community service and resilience. Off the slopes, Burns has a passion for baking and enjoys making cinnamon rolls and chocolate chip cookies.

    Note: With five World and Olympic titles,Burroughs is No. 2 in U.S. history, tied with Bruce Baumgartner, and just one behind John Smith at six
  • Won a U.S. record 69-straight matches to start his career on the Senior level from 2011-14
  • Won 2013 World title less than a month after breaking his ankle
  • sponsored by ASICS, Resilite, and CHOBANI.

Quick Facts
  • Son of Chris and Cheri Burrows
  • Has one brother, Jon
  • Has been skydiving three times
  • Buys motorcycles exclusively for road trips and sells them after the trip
  • Hobbies include fishing, hunting, golfing, surfing, traveling, free diving, spear fishing, archery, building fishing rods, backpacking and playing guitar
  • Grew up on a farm raising cows and chickens.
Career Highlights
  • 2019 World Cup Acapulco, 2nd (trap mixed), 22nd (trap)
  • 2015 World Cup Acapulco, 6th (trap)
  • 2015 World Cup Al Ain, 11th (trap)
  • 2013 World Cup Acapulco, 2nd (trap)
Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 bronze)
    • Olympic Games Tokyo 2020, bronze (Trap - Team - Mixed), 12th (Trap - Men)
World Championships Experience
  • Most recent: 2019 – 12th (Trap - Team - Mixed), 69th (Trap - Men)
  • Years of participation: Trap - Men 2019; Trap - Team - Mixed 2019
  • Top finish: 12th – 2019 (Trap - Team - Mixed)

bio.education: Skyline High School (Longmont, Colo.) ‘00 University of Colorado at Boulder, Economics; City University of Seattle '17, Applied Computing
bio.biography:
Quick Facts
  • Petty Officer First Class Burton was in his ninth year serving as an Arabic linguist with the United States Navy, including tours to Iraq and Kuwait, when in 2010 he was diagnosed with a degenerative eye disease called retinitis pigmentosa. Before medically retiring in February of 2011, Burton went through treatment at the VA Medical Center’s Augusta Blind Rehabilitation Center in Georgia, where he learned how to complete everyday tasks without his eyesight. An avid cross-country skier before losing his sight, Burton wore skis again for the first time in March 2012 at the VA’s National Disabled Veterans Winter Sports Clinic in Snowmass Village, Colorado. Burton competes with the aid of a guide who skis in front of him and gives him directions on the course. He competed at the Paralympic Winter Games Sochi 2014 in Nordic skiing. Following Sochi, he transitioned from Nordic skiing to alpine skiing
  • Son of Doug and Rene Burton
  • Has one sister, Brandi
  • Has two children, Ewan and Imogen
  • Fluent in Arabic
  • Hobbies include biking, computers, shooting, hiking and camping.
Paralympic Experience
  • Two-time Paralympian (2014, 2018)
  • PyeongChang 2018 Paralympic Winter Games, 5th (super combined), 7th (downhill), 9th (super-G)
  • Sochi 2014 Paralympic Winter Games, 9th (cross-country relay), 10th (cross-country long-distance), 12th (biathlon individual), 14th (cross-country sprint), 15th (cross-country middle-distance), 16th (biathlon sprint)
World Championship Experience
  • Most recent: 2017 – Alpine - 5th (super-G, super combined), 6th (downhill, slalom), 7th (giant slalom)
  • Years of Participation: 2017
  • Top Finish: 5th – 2017 (super-G, super combined)

Olympic Experience
  • 1-time Olympian; 1-time Olympic medalist (1 gold)
    • Olympic Games Paris 2024, gold (4 x 400m Relay - Women), 20th (400m - Women)
